The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, sometimes accompanied by subtle lip smacking. This is followed by motor manifestations that include bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, rhythmic jerking involving the limbs and entire body. Episodes feature asymmetric motor activity, including right arm flexion with left arm extension (adopting a fencer-like posture) and rightward head version with right arm extension. Involvement of the head and upper limbs is consistent, with clonic jerks evolving into generalized stiffening and jerking. Arm movements are often asymmetric, and leg flexion is present. All episodes culminate in unresponsiveness, with the patient becoming limp and unarousable at the end.

The patient exhibits a consistent pattern across multiple observations, beginning with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension in a fencer-like posture. This evolves into tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, then progresses to bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. In the final episode, the fencer posture was absent, with the event progressing directly from blank stare to rhythmic jerking. The patient remains unresponsive at the conclusion of each event.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset, followed by progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Initial features include subtle lip smacking in some episodes. Tonic stiffening involves the trunk and limbs, with arm extension and leg flexion observed during the tonic phase. This transitions into clonic jerking of the limbs and torso, characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous shaking that persists throughout the motor phase. Movements are bilateral without clear unilateral predominance and involve the entire body. The patient remains unresponsive during the episode, which concludes with postictal unresponsiveness and, in some cases, heavy breathing.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by automatisms including hand-to-face movements. This evolves into tonic stiffening involving the neck and upper limbs, with rightward head version and right arm extension, then progresses to bilateral clonic jerks affecting the face, arms, and legs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by tonic stiffening involving the trunk and limbs, with bilateral arm extension, leg flexion, clenched jaw, and upward eye deviation. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerks with rhythmic, synchronous shaking of the arms, legs, and face, including rhythmic jerking of all limbs and head. Some episodes show unilateral onset with right arm flexion and head version to the right, or fencer posturing (left arm flexion with right arm extension), which progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The jerking may be asynchronous in some instances, and tongue protrusion is observed. Breathing is preserved during the event, and the patient becomes unresponsive at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The clonic jerks involve the face and upper limbs, are bilateral and synchronous, with no clear unilateral predominance. No clear early signs or evolution of a seizure are visible prior to the observed events. The patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking, followed by rightward head version with right arm extension or flexion. This focal onset frequently evolves into rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks initially involving the right arm and leg, which then spread to the contralateral side, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In some episodes, the patient assumes a fencer posture with right arm flexion and left arm extension prior to generalized rhythmic jerking. The seizures are consistently associated with unresponsiveness during the event and transition into postictal unresponsiveness. Bilateral tonic–clonic activity is a common endpoint across episodes, with head and eyes often deviating to the right during the ictal phase.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, evolving into a tonic phase characterized by generalized stiffening of the trunk, limbs, and upper body, with clenched jaw and head and neck extension backward. During the tonic phase, the patient's head and eyes deviate to the right. This is followed by bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with shaking throughout the body. The episode concludes with a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness and slow, shallow breathing.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by subtle head version and in another by lip smacking. This progresses to tonic stiffening involving the trunk and limbs with bilateral arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks affecting the arms and legs. The episode evolves with fencer-like posturing, characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension, and includes a period of asynchronous shaking. The seizure concludes with a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness and slow, shallow breathing.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. The movements are asynchronous and involve the entire body, with no clear unilateral predominance. In one observed episode, the sequence began with right arm flexion and right leg extension in a fencer-like posture, followed by rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by one of several evolving patterns: (1) rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper limbs, or the face and limbs, bilaterally and asynchronously, without unilateral onset or tonic stiffening; (2) initial involvement of the right arm and leg with secondary generalization to bilateral tonic–clonic movements; (3) lip smacking preceding tonic stiffening and bilateral clonic jerks that progress to asynchronous shaking; or (4) tonic stiffening of the upper body and limbs evolving into bilateral clonic jerks with either rhythmic or asynchronous shaking, often accompanied by facial grimacing and mouth opening. In some episodes, there is rightward head version and right arm extension prior to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive during all events, which may conclude with postictal unresponsiveness and limpness.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle rightward head version. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs and facial grimacing. The episode ends with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by asynchronous, rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. The episode initially includes unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right before evolving into generalized motor activity. Automatisms such as hand-to-face gestures and persistent lip smacking are observed, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

The patient is initially sitting upright and talking on a phone when they suddenly recline backward in the chair. They begin to laugh, followed by eye rolling and opening the mouth wide in a scream. The episode progresses into a tonic phase with generalized stiffening, clenched jaw, and rigid body, accompanied by arms flailing outward and upward. This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk. The seizure evolves into a post-ictal state characterized by unresponsiveness and shallow breathing.
